items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Which store encodes mainly by sound?
items.why: It's Short-term memory. “Sensory register” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Atkinson & Shiffrin. “Bartlett” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Miller's magic number for STM capacity is:
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
